title: $3.5bn surplus, and slap for RBA
full_text:
Western Australian Labor has defied the Reserve Bank and pushed ahead with a fresh round of cash handouts, as Treasurer Rita Saffioti lashed out at Michele Bullock’s decision to hike interest rates.
Ms Saffioti, also the Deputy Premier, on Thursday unveiled the state’s eighth straight operating surplus, entrenching WA as the home of the strongest balance sheet in the nation.
The latest $3.5bn operating surplus – which exceeded the original $2.4bn surplus forecast a year ago – will help bankroll distributions to motorists and families with students amid ongoing cost-of-living pressures, as well as major investments aimed at delivering affordable housing.
Every holder of a driver’s licence in the state will receive a $100 fuel assistance payment, while the government’s third round of student assistance payments will be worth around $700 a year for households with two high school students.
But the state will also launch a major round of voluntary redundancies across the public service and cut its annual spending on consultants, travel and advertising by 5 per cent a year, as it looks to put a lid on government expenditure and salaries spending that have surged in recent years.
The budget’s forecast inflation rate has been increased from 3.25 per cent in December to 4 per cent, but Ms Saffioti downplayed suggestions that the latest handouts, as well as the ongoing major investment in infrastructure and housing around the state, were adding to inflation.
Instead, she attacked the Reserve Bank of Australia’s decision on Tuesday to increase interest rates, saying she believed it would undermine consumer confidence.
“I don’t believe Western Australians should have received an increase in the interest rate this week,” she said. “What we’ve seen in relation to the cost of living is a price spike as a result of the Iran war. We need to give confidence to the Western Australian community, and I think we need to support the Western Australian community. That’s my big focus.” Ms Saffioti said it was important for small businesses to still have customers with positivity about the future.
“I’m very passionate about that, this is a time to give Western Australians confidence and make sure that the small business, the small lunch bar, that they still have people walking through the door with confidence,” she said.
WA’s latest cash handouts were unveiled just a day after RBA governor Michele Bullock cautioned against such untargeted cost-of-living relief and warned that government handouts were making it harder to bring inflation under control.
The public service redundancies and cuts to discretionary spending follow an ongoing creep in government spending. The total amount spent on salaries is expected to break through $21.4bn in the coming year, up more than 50 per cent from 2023. Total government expenses will hit $52.5bn next year, up 43 per cent from four years ago.
Net debt will continue to rise despite the ongoing operating surpluses, given the state’s plans for a record $13.7bn investment in infrastructure next year and its forecast $44.3bn infrastructure spend over the next four years.
The budget includes substantial outlays on health infrastructure and housing initiatives.
WA’s net debt bottomed out in 2022-23 at $27.3bn but is now forecast to peak at $44.7bn in 2028-29.
The cuts to travel, consultants and advertising, the redundancy program, and the sale of surplus land will deliver $579m in savings over the next four years.
Ms Saffioti said the decision to offer voluntary redundancies across non-frontline roles around the public service was driven by feedback from department heads.
The budget included the first early sign that the federal government’s changes to the NDIS will force extra spending on to the states. The Thriving Kids initiative – agreed prior to the broader NDIS reforms flagged by Federal Health Minister Mark Butler – will see WA spend more than $300m on support for young school students with autism and other disabilities.
The budget does not include any extra expenditure as a result of the NDIS changes, which were announced after the budget was finalised, but the Cook government is privately resigned to having to bear a greater financial burden as a result of the federal changes.
The latest operating surplus will further agitate other states pushing for changes to the GST distribution methodology that has delivered around $6bn a year of extra money to WA since it was introduced by the Turnbull government in 2018.
Ms Saffioti said without the extra GST funding, WA would not be investing in the economic infrastructure needed to deliver the state’s next generation of developments – which will in turn become sources of federal tax revenue.
BDO indirect tax director Phil Renshaw said the budget had failed to deliver any meaningful relief or concessions for employers or changes to the state’s “inefficient and increasingly outdated” payroll tax regime.
“What is notable is what the budget does not do,” he said. “There are no substantive changes to payroll tax, no broader duty or land tax reform, and no changes to mining or energy royalties. For business, the message is one of stability and predictability – but also missed opportunity.”
summary: Western Australia has announced a $3.5 billion operating surplus, exceeding previous forecasts, and will distribute cash handouts to residents amid rising cost-of-living pressures. Treasurer Rita Saffioti criticized the Reserve Bank of Australia's recent interest rate hike, arguing it could harm consumer confidence. The budget also includes plans for public service redundancies and cuts to discretionary spending to manage rising government expenses.

heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: WA's operating surplus is $3.5 billion, surpassing forecasts.
Cash handouts include $100 for drivers and $700 for families with students.
Public service redundancies and spending cuts aim to control rising government costs.
relevance: This budget announcement impacts economic conditions and consumer behaviour, which are critical factors for actuaries and financial services professionals in their analyses and forecasts.
